PERF skip to_datetime cache True overhead for input shapes where it cannot help #### Summary to_datetime ... cache True the default unconditionally runs the _maybe_cache machinery Series dtype object construction + should_cache which iterates up to 500 elements of the input even on inputs where _convert_listlike_datetimes is going to dispatch to a non-parsing path or a path where caching has been measured to never help. On these inputs cache True is strictly slower than cache False by margins ranging from a few percent to 520 at the worst. All five no-help conditions are identifiable in O 1 time before any sampling just by looking at arg.dtype the unit kwarg and the format kwarg. #### Affected input shapes all O 1 to detect 1. unit is not None input is dispatched to _to_datetime_with_unit which is a vectorized cast caching cannot help. 2. arg.dtype is np.dtype M8 already datetime64 _convert_listlike_datetimes short-circuits. 3. arg.dtype is DatetimeTZDtype already tz-aware short-circuits. 4. arg.dtype is ArrowDtype with type is Timestamp short-circuits. 5. format is not None and format mixed array_strptime is fast enough that pd.unique + Series.map costs as much as just re-parsing cache True is never a measurable win on this path. #### Reproducer #### Measurements On main today n 100k unless noted Input cache False cache True Ratio --- --- --- --- unit s int 10 unique 0.07 ms 0.78 ms 11.0 slower unit s int n 100k all-unique 0.07 ms 0.92 ms 13.4 slower datetime64 ns ndarray 0.03 ms 0.07 ms 2.6 slower DatetimeIndex w tz any n 0.01 ms 6.15 ms 520 slower ArrowDtype timestamp any n 0.01 ms 0.64 ms 50 slower ISO + explicit format n 10k 100 unique 0.82 ms 1.19 ms 1.45 slower ISO + explicit format n 10k 10 unique 0.80 ms 1.26 ms 1.57 slower The DatetimeTZ case is the worst should_cache does set islice arg 500 which on a tz-aware DatetimeIndex materializes 500 Timestamp Python objects 6 ms and that cost is paid regardless of n . A to_datetime round-trip on an already-converted DatetimeIndex of any size is dominated entirely by this useless sampling step. #### Where the caching gate lives pandas core tools datetimes.py - _maybe_cache is called from the dispatch in to_datetime lines 1081 1090 1104 before _convert_listlike_datetimes runs its dtype short-circuits lines 367 412 . - should_cache lines 156 213 does the iteration that costs 6 ms on tz-aware DTIs. #### Proposed fix Add an O 1 early-bail at the top of _maybe_cache unit would need to be threaded from the dispatch sites currently bound in the partial only . No behavior change every one of these inputs hits a short-circuit non-parsing path in the next call. #### Out of scope separate follow-ups - format None on ISO strings where format inference succeeds strptime path. Same loss profile as the explicit-format case but only detectable by deferring the cache decision past format inference. - should_cache s unique_share 0.7 threshold is calibrated for the slow dateutil path and is too generous for fast-parse inputs that get past the O 1 gate. #### Pandas version pandas main 501e319b0b . | PERF Replace dateutil-based parser fallback with a regex-based parser in tslibs.parsing In non-ISO8601 cases we use dateutil s parser but this is 1 not-optimized pure-python and 2 parses some strings that we probably should raise on e.g. Jan 10am 5th 1234 The hard part here is deciding exactly what cases we want to allow and a deprecation cycle for cases that we disallow. Using a regex with a fixed set of formats would be significantly faster preliminary benchmarks are 5-15x on cases with month name sand 100x on cases with AM or PM . Running list of issues that this might address #17265 | API how to handle time zone DST transitions in the distant future Historically by using pytz by default for time zones tz-aware data had wrong offsets for DST periods after 2038 e.g. see and and documented at scroll down to the 5th warning box sidenote we should update this . The same happens if you were using dateutil.tz time zones and the short summary is that pytz and dateutil are only parsing the Version 1 TZif files those don t include a rule for future times and therefore typically include transitions times extrapolated to the future but because of 32bit are limited to dates up to 2038. This issue was fixed by switching to zoneinfo by default in pandas 3.0 since zoneinfo supports the newer versions of TZif files and can use a rule-based logic for determining the offset for distant dates. However then after improving the performance for zoneinfo timezones to also use our internal fast binary search of the transition points in pandas 3.0.3 this changed this behaviour again because now we have to pre-populate our array of transition dates to use for the binary search and the linked PR decided to do that up to 2100 meaning we only get correct offsets up to 2100 and not for later dates see the example below . In I am extending that cut-off from 2100 to 2262. While having tz-aware data so far in the future is a bit a debatable use case having an arbitrary cut off after which it behaves differently is not ideal. Options - Decide we are OK with an arbitrary cut-off if it is far enough in the future e.g. 2262 and document this - Populate the transition data dynamically based on the range of the input data eg by default fill up to year 2100 but if the input data max year is 2150 we recalculate it up to that date and update the cached data - Use our fast-path for a normal range eg up to 2100 to cover most use cases and then for dates after that use the zoneinfo python API directly as a slower fallback -- Code illustration With pytz default in pandas 3 With dateutil With zoneinfo in pandas 2.3 or 3.0.0 - 3.0.2 With default zoneinfo in pandas 3.0.3

BUG DataFrame created from Index or Series unexpectedly changes the original object ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description When a DataFrame is created from an existing Index or Series modifying the newly created DataFrame can unexpectedly affect the original object. In the example above the new DataFrame is created from a copy of the original columns but changing a value inside the new object still changes the column labels of the original DataFrame . This behavior is surprising because the derived object should be independent especially when Copy-on-Write mode is enabled. ### Expected Behavior Updating the derived DataFrame should not modify the original DataFrame or its Index. Users who want to optimize memory must - Manually inspect each column s dtype - Know the integer float downcast rules by heart - Manually check cardinality for object columns - Write their own loops to apply changes This is a very common pain point especially for data engineers and analysts working with medium-to-large datasets who hit memory pressure and have no built-in tool to diagnose it. ### Feature Description A new method DataFrame.memory_report that 1. Analyzes every column s current dtype and actual value range 2. Suggests the smallest safe dtype for each column 3. Explains why each suggestion is safe value range cardinality ratio 4. Returns a rich MemoryReport object that is both human-readable and 5. machine-readable 6. Never modifies the DataFrame purely diagnostic zero side effects def memory_report self verbose bool True category_threshold float 0.5 check_datetimes bool True - MemoryReport Analyze memory usage and suggest safe dtype optimizations. Parameters ---------- verbose bool default True If True print the full report to stdout. category_threshold float default 0.5 Suggest category dtype when unique ratio is below this value. For example 0.5 means fewer than 50 unique values suggest category. check_datetimes bool default True Attempt to detect datetime-parseable strings in object columns. Samples up to 200 rows to avoid scanning very large columns. Returns ------- MemoryReport Structured report with per-column diagnostics. Supports - report.to_dataframe machine-readable table - report.apply df apply all suggestions returns new df - report.actionable list of columns with suggestions - report.high_impact columns with 50 savings potential ### Alternative Solutions df.info memory_usage deep . This is the closest native.But no guidance on how to fix it. ### Additional Context Design Decisions Zero side effects. memory_report never modifies the DataFrame. Applying suggestions is always an explicit opt-in via report.apply df . Safe-only suggestions. Every suggestion is lossless by default Integer downcasts are validated against actual min max values Float32 suggestions are validated values are cast to float32 and checked for overflow before recommending apply wraps each conversion in a try except and warns on failure it never raises Null awareness. The report flags columns with nulls so users know before applying dtype changes that may affect null representation. Overflow guard for floats. The implementation detects when a finite float64 value would become inf after float32 cast and keeps float64. Composable return type.
